source: Invest Like the Best — The cyclone under the surface (EP.260)
summary: Software is the 'consumer staple of tech' — fundamentals will be most resilient in a slowing economy
summary_extended: Baker argues that software is the 'consumer staple of tech,' meaning it offers the most resilient and stable fundamentals relative to other tech subsectors during an economic slowdown. Unlike internet companies facing COVID-era demand normalization headwinds (pull-forward reversals), enterprise software has no comparable hangover and benefits from structural, non-discretionary spending patterns. He cites CIO surveys and JP Morgan commentary as evidence that cloud spending is actually accelerating into 2022, not decelerating. Baker expresses strong conviction — using superlatives like 'really, really bullish' — and specifically favors free-cash-flow-generating software names trading at 2-4% FCF yields on 2023 estimates as the preferred entry point. The investment implication is to overweight profitable, FCF-positive software companies as a defensive-growth position in a slowing macro environment.
detail: Baker is 'really, really bullish on software' and views it as having the most stable relative fundamentals in a slowdown. Unlike internet companies suffering COVID hangovers, software has no comparable demand normalization headwind. CIO surveys and JP Morgan commentary support accelerating cloud spend in 2022. Baker has a strong bias toward free-cash-flow-generating software names, particularly those with 2-4% FCF yields looking to 2023.
confidence_evidence: Baker uses superlatives ('really, really bullish'), cites JP Morgan commentary, CIO surveys, and explicitly contrasts with internet weakness.
quote_fragment: software is the consumer staple of tech...best relative fundamentals
